moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening, a flexible and strategic system that avoids the sharp theory of 1.e4. By controlling d5 and preparing to expand on the queenside, we establish a modest yet solid foothold in the center. This move is especially popular among players who prefer maneuvering and long-term planning over tactical complications—you'll often see it in games by Kasparov and Karpov, who used it to sidestep opponents' prepared defenses.

Black plays the Adorjan Defense, signaling an intention to fianchetto the kingside bishop to g7. By preparing this setup, Black aims for flexibility and counterplay on the long diagonal—the g7 bishop will eye our center and the c3 square. This move announces a hypermodern approach: rather than stake a claim in the center immediately, Black will pressure ours from afar.

We seize the center decisively, occupying e4 and supporting our c4 pawn while gaining space. This move shifts our strategy from pure maneuvering to establishing central control, which fits well against Black's patient setup. Now we enjoy more space and central presence, though we must be ready for Black's counterattacking ideas on the long diagonal and queenside.

Black challenges our center head-on rather than sitting passively. By playing e5, Black stakes a claim in the center and gains the option to develop pieces actively while our e4 pawn is under some pressure. This is a principled response that prevents us from playing entirely on our own terms—watch for Black to follow up with moves like d6, Bg7, and active piece play to justify this central advance.
